food_subgroup: Noodle soup — prawn-pork bone broth with yellow noodles and rice vermicelli, prawns, pork ribs, egg, water spinach, lard croutons

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Hokkien Prawn Mee Soup as a prepared Singapore hawker dish. Closest parent would be a generic noodle soup concept; no exact or near-exact FoodOn term available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup, prawn-pork broth (closest parent match — no exact FoodEx2 code for Hokkien Prawn Mee Soup)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest defensible parent for a prawn-based noodle soup. No exact FoodEx2 entry for this Singapore hawker preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for this specific prepared hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of Hokkien Prawn Mee Soup weighs approximately 700g, comprising ~120g cooked mixed noodles (yellow noodles ~80g + bee hoon ~40g), ~350ml prawn-pork bone broth, ~60g pork ribs (2 pieces), ~50g prawns (3 pieces), ~25g half hard-boiled egg, kangkung, and lard croutons.

nutrient_source.title: Hokkien Prawn Mee Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database for prawn noodle soup (hae mee), supplemented by component-level calculation for the specific hawker bowl composition (yellow noodles + bee hoon + prawn-pork broth + pork ribs + prawns + egg + kangkung + lard croutons). Per-100g values represent the weighted composite of all components at the stated portion weights. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogues where HPB data was unavailable.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Noodle Dishes — Yellow Egg Noodle and Rice Vermicelli Weighted Estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~58 derived as a weighted average of yellow egg noodle (GI ~60, University of Sydney GI Database) and rice vermicelli / bee hoon (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate cooked mass ratio (80g yellow noodles : 40g bee hoon = 2:1). Protein from prawns, pork ribs, and egg moderates postprandial glucose response. GL ~30.2 calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (58 × 52.1) / 100 ≈ 30.2, where available carbohydrate per 700g serving = 7.9 × 7 = 55.3g minus fibre 0.4 × 7 = 2.8g, net ~52.1g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium — approximately 1,050mg per bowl from prawn-pork bone broth base (~600mg) plus noodles, pork ribs, and lard croutons; rises to ~1,250mg with full sambal
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should leave at least half the broth unconsumed to reduce sodium intake by approximately 300mg, skip sambal to avoid a further ~200mg sodium, and request the dish without lard croutons; even with these modifications, a single bowl contributes 35–50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-to-high glycemic load (~30 per bowl) from ~55g available carbohydrate in mixed yellow noodles and bee hoon, partially offset by protein from prawns, pork ribs, and egg
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this as a high-carbohydrate meal (GL ~30 approaches the upper moderate threshold for a single meal) and consider requesting a smaller noodle portion, pairing with additional protein, and monitoring postprandial glucose; the mixed noodle base (GI ~58) is preferable to pure white rice dishes but still requires portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1,050mg per bowl), saturated fat from lard croutons and pork ribs (~6.3g per bowl), and dietary cholesterol (~154mg per bowl) from egg, prawns, and pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, request the dish without lard croutons to reduce saturated fat by approximately 3–4g, leave the broth unfinished to reduce sodium, and note that the prawn-pork broth contributes significant dietary cholesterol; occasional consumption with broth reduction is preferable to regular full-bowl intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (~1,050mg per bowl) is the primary concern for cardiovascular comorbidity management in RA; moderate saturated fat from lard croutons and pork ribs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware of the high sodium content and its implications for cardiovascular comorbidity; prawns provide trace omega-3 (~0.2g per 50g serving) and the prawn shell-derived broth contributes iodine, but these benefits do not offset the sodium burden at full-bowl consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hokkien Prawn Mee Soup (虾面汤, Hae Mee) is a quintessential Singapore hawker noodle dish with deep Hokkien Chinese culinary roots. The defining element is its intensely flavoured prawn-pork bone broth, prepared by slow-simmering fried prawn heads and shells together with pork ribs, rock sugar, and aromatics for up to four hours, producing a rich, coral-coloured liquid with complex umami depth derived from prawn shell astaxanthin and iodine. A standard hawker bowl (~700g) contains a mixture of yellow alkaline noodles (~80g cooked) and thin rice vermicelli or bee hoon (~40g cooked), topped with two pieces of pork ribs (~60g), three blanched prawns (~50g), half a hard-boiled egg (~25g), water spinach (kangkung), and crispy lard croutons. The dish is served with sambal chilli on the side. Nutritionally, one bowl provides approximately 515 kcal, 26g protein, 18g fat (including ~6g saturated fat from lard and pork), and 55g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at ~1,050mg per bowl without sambal (~1,250mg with sambal), making it a significant concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is approximately 58 (medium) due to the mixed noodle base, with a glycemic load of ~30 per bowl. The dish is NOT halal (contains lard and pork) and is dairy-free. Famous reference stalls include 545 Whampoa Prawn Mee and Blanco Court Prawn Mee in Singapore. Clinically, this dish is high-sodium, moderate-GL, and moderate in saturated fat; portion reduction of broth and noodles is the primary dietary modification strategy.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes) with component-level calculation for the specific bowl composition. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI are all supported by approved sources. The dish is a well-documented Singapore hawker staple with consistent preparation across reference stalls.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite hawker dish (prawn-pork broth + mixed noodles + lard croutons) is not available as a single entry in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; component-level MUFA/PUFA data would require individual ingredient disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as served (including broth) is not reported as a single per-100g figure in HPB Singapore Foodo. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore source for this dish preparation. Copper (0.05mg/100g), manganese (0.06mg/100g), and vitamin_k (12.0mcg/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analogues (yellow egg noodles, cooked shrimp, pork ribs, hard-boiled egg, water spinach) where HPB data was unavailable; confidence 0.9. GI ~58 is an analogous estimate weighted from University of Sydney GI Database values for yellow egg noodle (GI ~60) and rice vermicelli (GI ~53). GL ~30.2 calculated from net available carbohydrate per 700g serving. The dish is NOT halal due to lard croutons and pork ribs. Sodium per bowl (~1,050mg) is based on the full bowl including broth; leaving broth unfinished substantially reduces sodium intake. FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 or FoodOn code exists for this specific Singapore hawker preparation.
